---
title: "Concepts and Datasets"
description: "Distinguish AI concepts and identify features, labels, and datasets."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [machine-learning-ai]
order: 640
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Beginner to intermediate
**Estimated time:** 35–50 minutes
**You will learn:** Distinguish AI concepts and identify features, labels, and datasets.
**Practice in:** Jupyter or Google Colab
:::

## Why this matters

Distinguish AI concepts and identify features, labels, and datasets.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Concepts and Datasets** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Learning objectives"}
You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. AI and ML Concepts** — Distinguish AI, machine learning, deep learning, and models in beginner language.
- **2. Features, Labels, and Datasets** — Identify inputs, targets, rows, and columns in supervised learning data.

## 1. AI and ML Concepts

Distinguish AI, machine learning, deep learning, and models in beginner language.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A model is like a student trained with practice questions; it may improve, but it can
still misunderstand new questions.
:::

### What this means

Machine learning systems learn patterns from data examples instead of following only
hand-written rules.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
terms = {"AI": "broad field", "ML": "learns from data", "model": "pattern machine"}
print(terms)
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `terms = {"AI": "broad field", "ML": "learns from data", "model": "patte...` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `print(terms)` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
AI output can sound confident even when wrong. Evaluate results instead of trusting
tone.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "machine-learning-ai-ai-ml-concepts",
  "title": "AI and ML Concepts checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes AI and ML Concepts?",
      "options": [
        "Machine learning systems learn patterns from data examples instead of following only hand-written rules.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Machine learning systems learn patterns from data examples instead of following only hand-written rules."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A model is like a student trained with practice questions; it may improve, but it can still misunderstand new questions."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Features, Labels, and Datasets

Identify inputs, targets, rows, and columns in supervised learning data.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
Features are clues in a case file; the label is the answer written on solved cases.
:::

### What this means

Features are input measurements; labels are the answers a supervised model learns to
predict.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
features = [[5.1, 3.5], [6.2, 3.4]]
labels = ["setosa", "versicolor"]
print(features[0], labels[0])
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `features = [[5.1, 3.5], [6.2, 3.4]]` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `labels = ["setosa", "versicolor"]` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `print(features[0], labels[0])` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Leaking the answer into the features makes a model look better than it really is.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "machine-learning-ai-features-labels-datasets",
  "title": "Features, Labels, and Datasets checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Features, Labels, and Datasets?",
      "options": [
        "Features are input measurements; labels are the answers a supervised model learns to predict.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Features are input measurements; labels are the answers a supervised model learns to predict."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Features are clues in a case file; the label is the answer written on solved cases."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
first example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- **AI and ML Concepts**: Machine learning systems learn patterns from data examples instead of following only
hand-written rules.
- **Features, Labels, and Datasets**: Features are input measurements; labels are the answers a supervised model learns to
predict.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- scikit-learn documentation: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/
- PyTorch documentation: https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/index.html
- TensorFlow documentation: https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs
- Hugging Face Transformers documentation: https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
